manipulated_prompt_obj.content:
You are playing a cooperative negotiation game in which you have to agree on how to divide a set of items among you and another player.

Rules:
(a) You and the other player are given a set of items. Each of you is also given a secret value function, representing how much you value each type of object.
(b) You exchange messages with the other player to agree on who gets which items. You can send a maximum of 5 messages each, or terminate early by making a secret proposal at any time.
(c) You are each asked to submit a secret proposal indicating the items you want formatted in square brackets as follows: "[Proposal: <number> <object name>, <number> <object name>, <...>]"
(d) If your proposals are complementary, i.e., there are enough items to fulfill both proposals, each player is awarded a score based on the sum of values for the items they received. Otherwise, both of you get zero points.
(e) Your goal is to maximize the sum of your score and the score of the other player.

Let us start.

The set of available items is:

3 scissors, 1 thimble, 1 fork

Your secret value function is:

scissors: 3, thimble: 0, fork: 1

IMPORTANT: Your messages, unless it is the secret proposal, are directly transmitted to the other player, so do not include any response to the rules or text announcing your message. To make a secret proposal, use the indicated format. Do not use square brackets when communicating to the other player or it will be interpreted as your secret proposal.

message.content: I propose I take the three scissors, and you take the fork and the thimble. Is that acceptable?

The user wants to simulate a cooperative negotiation game: the player (the assistant) and a "other player" must agree on division of items given the set of items and each player's secret value function. We need to exchange messages with the other player, with a maximum of 5 messages each. The goal: maximize sum of both players' scores (cooperative). If proposals are complementary (i.e., they don't conflict; there are enough items to satisfy both), each receives a score based on sum of values for the items they get. If proposals conflict (i.e., they demand more of an item than available, or assign same item to both), both get zero.

We are the assistant. We must generate messages (not in square brackets, because that's reserved for secret proposal). But we can propose items we want. The other player will respond with messages, maybe propose their own desires. Then after at most 5 messages each, we may submit a secret proposal in the required format: "[Proposal: <number> <object name>, <number> <object name>, <...>]". The secret proposals are not shown to other player until both submit. If they are complementary, each gets the items, and scores computed accordingly. If not, both get zero.

We need to figure out what the other player's secret value function is. It is not given, but presumably we can try to infer or assume typical values.
